sourcematerial: '''Page 1: "We use the Gulf of Alaska sockeye salmon fishery to show how attribution timeseries may be used to contextualize the impacts of human-induced ocean warming on ecosystem services. We link negative warming effects on sockeye fishery catches to increasing human influence on regional temperatures (increasing FAR values), and we find that sockeye salmon migrating to sea in years with the strongest evidence for human effects on temperature (FAR ≥ 0.98) produce catches 1.4 standard deviations below the long-term log mean." Page 4: "All five regions, plus the overall North Pacific, have reached temperatures that can be attributed to human activities with high confidence (i.e. FAR > 0.98). In particular, North Pacific SST has effectively become fixed in an anthropogenic state, with FAR⩾ 0.99 for three-year running mean SST since 2003, and for annual SST since 2013 (figure 3)."'''

More Notes: Annual SST

sourcematerial:
The source material provided includes the following relevant excerpts for justifying the quantitative results:
- "Attributing time series track the evolution of human influence on sea surface temperature, showing overwhelming evidence of human contribution by 2014–2020." (Page 1)
- "We link negative warming effects on sockeye fishery catches to increasing human influence on regional temperatures (increasing FAR values), and we find that sockeye salmon migrating to sea in years with the strongest evidence for human effects on temperature (FAR ≥ 0.98) produce catches 1.4 standard deviations below the long-term log mean." (Page 1)
- "Increased North Pacific temperatures, particularly SST extremes, are driving changes in sockeye salmon productivity." (Page 9)
- The source material on page 6 indicates that extreme SST anomalies that were exceedingly rare in preindustrial times, occurring every 1000–10,000 years are expected every 5–40 years in the current climate. It specifically mentions that the eastern Bering Sea experiences large SST anomalies expected every ~40 years, with the Gulf of Alaska seeing them every ~5 years, in the current climate.
- "The analysis uses Bayesian estimates of the Fraction of Attributable Risk (FAR) and Risk Ratio (RR) derived from 23 global climate models...The analysis highlights human influence on North Pacific sea surface temperatures as the main driver of risk with implications for ecosystem services." (Page 5)
- "A rapid increase in SST risk ratios and the shift from rare to common thresholds for extreme temperatures over recent decades." (Page 7)

These excerpts provide evidence that the increased frequency of SST anomalies in the eastern Bering Sea and the Gulf of Alaska, as well as their implications on sockeye salmon catches, are a result of human-induced climate change, as outlined in the source material.

More Notes: Midpoint of the period 2014–2020.  Annual SST anomalies this large expected every 5 years in current climate, compared to 1000–10,000 years in preindustrial climate.
